concerns.text: The core validation step (FR-005, SC-001) proposes correlating 'per-sample SHAP importance scores' (derived from a GNN trained to predict local heat flux) against 'global thermal conductivity'. This is a circular/tautological validation. The GNN is trained to minimize error in predicting local heat flux, which is the integrand of the Green-Kubo formula used to compute the global thermal conductivity. Therefore, the 'feature importance' of the GNN is mathematically determined by the same physical quantity (heat flux) that defines the target variable. Correlating the model's explanation of its own training target against the target itself is not an independent validation of topology's influence; it is a self-fulfilling prophecy that will inevitably show high correlation if the model converges, regardless of whether topology is the true causal driver.

The plan trains a GNN to predict 'local heat flux' using only static topological metrics (degree, clustering) as input features. In amorphous solids, local heat flux is a dynamic quantity dependent on atomic velocities and time-correlated forces, not just static topology. A model trained on static topology to predict dynamic flux will likely fail to converge or learn spurious correlations. If it does converge, the resulting 'feature importance' reflects the model's ability to fit noise or the limited variance in the static features, not a physical causal link. The analysis assumes a static-to-dynamic mapping that is physically ill-posed without time-resolved inputs, rendering the resulting correlation scientifically meaningless.

The plan proposes a Pearson correlation between 'per-sample SHAP importance scores' and 'global thermal conductivity' with N=10 samples. SHAP values are high-dimensional (one per metric per atom, aggregated per sample). Aggregating these into a single 'importance score' per sample introduces significant noise. With N=10, the statistical power to detect a correlation between these noisy, derived aggregates and the global property is negligible. The Bonferroni correction will likely render any p-value non-significant, or conversely, if a correlation is found, it is likely a false positive due to overfitting the small sample size during the GNN training phase.
